attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for a general check-up. Her past medical history includes sub-optimally controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus (20-year history), hypertension and osteoarthritis. Over the last five years she has developed chronic kidney disease. Her current medications include telmisartan, chlorthalidone, metoprolol, and insulin. She has increased her non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) use for the past few weeks due to bilateral knee pain.

On examination her blood pressure is 132/80 mmHg, pulse 72/min, temperature 37.4oC, respiratory rate 16/min and SpO2 97% on room air. Her jugular venous pressure is 2cm. She has vesicular breath sounds throughout the chest. She has no peripheral oedema.

Laboratory tests reveal the following:

  • Sodium 138 mmol/L (135-145)

  • Potassium 4.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)

  • Chloride 104 mmol/L (98-108)

  • Bicarbonate 19 mmol/L (22-32)

  • Creatinine = 310 mmol/L (60-110)

  • White blood cell count 7.9 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)

  • Hemoglobin 88 g/L (120-160)

On her last visit three months ago, her creatinine was 230 mmol/L.
attributes.stemQuestion: Prior to rechecking her renal function in two weeks, which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Commence prophylactic antibiotics for urinary tract infection
attributes.explanation:
The most likely cause of this acute-on-chronic kidney injury is her increased use of NSAIDs for knee pain. Discontinuing NSAIDs is the most appropriate next step in management. NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, which reduces renal blood flow and glomerular filtration rate (GFR). In patients with CKD, the kidneys are dependent on prostaglandins to maintain adequate perfusion. NSAIDs can therefore precipitate acute kidney injury, particularly in patients with pre-existing risk factors such as diabetes, hypertension, and CKD - all of which this patient has. The temporal relationship between increased NSAID use and worsening renal function strongly suggests this as the cause.

Discontinuing telmisartan (an angiotensin receptor blocker) is incorrect as this is unlikely to be the cause of the patient's acute kidney injury, given this is a regular medication of the patient. The stem does not indicate that this medicine was recently commenced or recently dose-increased. ACE inhibitors / ARBs provide nephroprotection in patients with diabetic kidney disease, and discontinuation could lead to worsening proteinuria and progression of CKD in the long term.

Commencing oral sodium bicarbonate may be appropriate for patients with metabolic acidosis due to CKD. While this patient does have a low bicarbonate level (19 mmol/L), addressing the likely cause of the acute deterioration (NSAIDs) is the priority. Sodium bicarbonate therapy could be considered later if metabolic acidosis persists after discontinuing nephrotoxic medications.

Commencing prophylactic antibiotics for urinary tract infection is not indicated as there are no symptoms or signs of urinary tract infection. The white blood cell count is normal, and there is no fever or urinary symptoms mentioned. Unnecessary antibiotic use can lead to resistance and adverse effects.

Commencing oral sodium chloride tablets would not be beneficial and could potentially be harmful. This patient already has CKD and hypertension, and increasing sodium intake could worsen her blood pressure control and fluid status.

After discontinuing NSAIDs, alternative pain management strategies for her osteoarthritis should be discussed, such as paracetamol, physiotherapy, and weight management. Her renal function should be closely monitored, and if it does not improve after discontinuing NSAIDs, further investigation for other causes of acute kidney injury would be warranted.
learningPoints: NSAIDs can worsen kidney function in patients with chronic kidney disease by reducing renal blood flow.
Patients with diabetes, hypertension, and pre-existing kidney disease are at higher risk for NSAID-induced acute kidney injury.
Management of acute-on-chronic kidney injury includes review of nephrotoxic medications and close monitoring of renal function.
references: Dixit M, Doan T, Kirschner R, et al. Significant Acute Kidney Injury Due to Non-steroidal Anti-inflammatory Drugs: Inpatient Setting. Pharmaceuticals (Basel). 2010;3(4):1279-1285
